coredata.dc:description: Enteromorpha Prolifera (EP), a non-native macroalgae, has significantly damaged coastal agricultural ecosystems through its rapid expansion. Thus, developing an eco-friendly and cost-effective waste conversion strategy is urgently required. To address this issue, this study presents a hydrothermal-assisted multifunctional product preparation (HMP) strategy, aimed at enhancing the value-added processing of EP. EP biochar (EBC) was initially employed to coordinate the degradation of tetracycline (TC) by BiOCl. The introduction of EBC effectively modified the energy bands and electrical properties of BiOCl. The results demonstrated that BiOCl@EBC significantly enhanced the degradation efficiency of TC (from 57.34 % to 99.46 % within 60 min). The liquid by-product was valorized to synthesize blue carbon dots (CDs) through a facile process, enabling the fabrication of fluorescent anti-counterfeiting patterns; these CDs demonstrate excitation-wavelength-tunable emission. This study presents a sustainable approach to valorize waste EP into EBC and CDs, while developing an eco-friendly modification strategy for BiOCl photocatalysts. The HMP strategy enables valorization of waste into high-value functional products while establishing a sustainable conversion framework for algal.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: High-temperature pyrolysis is the primary method for producing EP biochar, but it generates gas emissions. In contrast, hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) effectively avoids this issue (Kumar et al., 2018). Furthermore, HTC utilizes pure water as a solvent, making them environmentally friendly and enhancing the surface functional groups of the carbonized solids (Petrovi\u0107 et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2019b). Hydrothermal EBC is more suitable for the preparation of support adsorbent materials (Mu et al., 2024; Tan and Zhou, 2024; Zhang et al., 2019a). Besides, the liquid product after Hydrothermal processes could be the raw material for the preparation of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). CDs have been widely used in biosensing, solar energy conversion, and optical anti-counterfeiting applications due to their excellent optical properties (Kalytchuk et al., 2018; Karako\u00E7ak et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2018; Zuo et al., 2018). Yet, fluorescent anti-counterfeiting materials still confront the problems of low-cost, large-scale production and rapid preparation of anti-counterfeiting patterns (Ren et al., 2022). Given these challenges, HTC can effectively balance material preparation costs and carbon emissions, promoting the sustainable and efficient use of EP in an environmentally friendly manner.
This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 42306168), the Science and Technology Program of Dinghai District (No. 2022C31001).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Enteromorpha Prolifera (EP), a non-native macroalgae, has significantly damaged coastal agricultural ecosystems through its rapid expansion. Thus, developing an eco-friendly and cost-effective waste conversion strategy is urgently required. To address this issue, this study presents a hydrothermal-assisted multifunctional product preparation (HMP) strategy, aimed at enhancing the value-added processing of EP. EP biochar (EBC) was initially employed to coordinate the degradation of tetracycline (TC) by BiOCl. The introduction of EBC effectively modified the energy bands and electrical properties of BiOCl. The results demonstrated that BiOCl@EBC significantly enhanced the degradation efficiency of TC (from 57.34 % to 99.46 % within 60 min). The liquid by-product was valorized to synthesize blue carbon dots (CDs) through a facile process, enabling the fabrication of fluorescent anti-counterfeiting patterns; these CDs demonstrate excitation-wavelength-tunable emission. This study presents a sustainable approach to valorize waste EP into EBC and CDs, while developing an eco-friendly modification strategy for BiOCl photocatalysts. The HMP strategy enables valorization of waste into high-value functional products while establishing a sustainable conversion framework for algal.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The swift advancement of the medical industry has resulted in the release of substantial quantities of antibiotics such as tetracycline (TC) into aquatic environments, thereby inevitably impacting both the ecology and water quality (Lin et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2023b; Chen et al., 2023). To address this environmental challenge, the synthesis of efficient photocatalysts for the degradation of antibiotics has emerged as a crucial strategy in environmental management. Among the various catalysts developed, bismuth-based oxides, such as BiOCl, have demonstrated significant efficacy in degrading these antibiotic molecules (Lin et al., 2021b; Su et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2022c; Yosefi and Haghighi, 2018). However, the degradation efficiency of BiOCl was hindered by energy band mismatch and limited absorbance (Sharma et al., 2019). To address these issues, researchers have developed several strategies aimed at optimizing the degradation properties of these materials. These strategies include narrowing the band gap and increasing the number of active sites (Sun et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2022a). Additionally, the use of support materials has been identified as an efficient method for optimizing the catalytic degradation process (Lin et al., 2021a). The S group leveraged the high specific surface area and superior adsorption capacity of zeolite to enhance the active sites of Bi2Sn2O7-C3N4, significantly improving its degradation efficiency (Heidari et al., 2018). Similarly, two-dimensional materials like graphene offer unique advantages in facilitating catalytic degradation (Jiang et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2021). However, the complex processes and high costs of these auxiliary materials hinder their large-scale production. Developing eco-friendly auxiliary materials at a lower cost remains challenging. In this context, conveniently prepared biochar emerges as a highly promising candidate due to its excellent adsorption capacity and diverse surface groups, making it particularly suitable for assisted catalytic degradation (Kang et al., 2023).

Natural biomass materials have the potential to be converted into biochar. Algae biochar holds significant promise due to its abundant raw materials and low production costs. Enteromorpha Prolifera (EP), non-native macroalgae, poses a significant threat to offshore ecosystems due to its remarkable capacity for rapid expansion (Jaanika et al., 2002; Liu et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2022b). EP has diverse applications, including fuels, dyes, fibers, and food (González Fernández et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2016; Trivedi et al., 2013; Wu et al., 2023). Obtaining these by-products involves complex reprocessing. To fully harness the potential of EP, the development of effective methods is essential, with EP biochar (EBC) emerging as a viable solution to enhance the efficiency of BiOCl degradation.

High-temperature pyrolysis is the primary method for producing EP biochar, but it generates gas emissions. In contrast, hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) effectively avoids this issue (Kumar et al., 2018). Furthermore, HTC utilizes pure water as a solvent, making them environmentally friendly and enhancing the surface functional groups of the carbonized solids (Petrović et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2019b). Hydrothermal EBC is more suitable for the preparation of support adsorbent materials (Mu et al., 2024; Tan and Zhou, 2024; Zhang et al., 2019a). Besides, the liquid product after Hydrothermal processes could be the raw material for the preparation of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). CDs have been widely used in biosensing, solar energy conversion, and optical anti-counterfeiting applications due to their excellent optical properties (Kalytchuk et al., 2018; Karakoçak et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2018; Zuo et al., 2018). Yet, fluorescent anti-counterfeiting materials still confront the problems of low-cost, large-scale production and rapid preparation of anti-counterfeiting patterns (Ren et al., 2022). Given these challenges, HTC can effectively balance material preparation costs and carbon emissions, promoting the sustainable and efficient use of EP in an environmentally friendly manner.

In this study, guided by the "Waste to Wealth" principle, we devised a hydrothermal-assisted multifunctional products preparation (HMP) strategy, a facile one-pot hydrothermal method to efficiently convert discarded shoreline EP into biochar and CDs. EBC possesses diverse surface groups and exhibits excellent adsorption capacity. Additionally, it improves the electrical properties and optimizes the energy band structure of BiOCl. EBC led to the formation of a novel catalyst, BiOCl@EBC, which significantly improved degradation efficiency from 57.34 % to 99.49 %. EBC demonstrated promise as a cost-effective auxiliary material actively involved in degrading the TC process, eliminating the need for a conventional activation process. Additionally, we created fluorescent ECDs by utilizing liquid products obtained from HTC. This method allows for the commercialization, eco-friendly, and rapid application of anti-counterfeiting patterns through spraying. Our study enhances the versatility of EP treatments and guides for the preparation of EP-modified BiOCl and CDs anti-counterfeit patterns by effectively utilizing hydrothermal products. Additionally, we demonstrated the versatility of our experimental program in utilizing different natural resources and promoted the concept of waste utilization.

Fig. 1 illustrates the process of preparing ECDs of EBC. EP from Zhoushan, East China Sea. EP was washed, dried, and ground into powder followed by taking 15 g of EP powder and mixing it with 40 mL of purified water. The EP solution was loaded into a Teflon-lined autoclave (80 mL) for hydrothermal reaction with different temperatures (140 °C–260 °C) and time (5 h or 10 h). Separation of solid and liquid products by centrifugation (8000 r/10 min) at the end of the hydrothermal process, EBC is the dried solid.

The collected liquid product was further subjected to centrifugation operation and the supernatant was taken and then filtered three times through 0.22 μm membranes. The liquid is subsequently dialyzed to obtain ECDs solution. The ECDs solution can be diluted directly with high-purity water for spraying, or the ECDs solution could be dried at a low temperature (60 °C/4 h) into powder for characterization.

Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP), saturated potassium chloride (KCl), and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (2 mmol) were dissolved in 0.1 mol/L of mannitol, followed by the injection of 0.4 g of polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP). Saturated potassium chloride (KCl) solution had been prepared (10 mL). These solutions were mixed well by ultrasonication for 30 min and stirred for 30 min at room temperature. Next, the solutions were heated to 160 °C for 3 h in an oven. After natural cooling, the BiOCl power was attained after sufficient cleaning with ethanol and deionized water. During BiOCl preparation, 5, 10, 15, and 20 mg of biochar were added respectively, and the obtained samples were labeled as BiOCl@EBC (5 mg), BiOCl@EBC (10 mg), BiOCl@EBC (15 mg), and BiOCl@EBC (20 mg).

Photocatalytic degradation antibiotic experiments were launched in a 100-mL photochemical reactor with a 300-W xenon lamp (λ > 420 nm) as the sunlight source. Typically, 20 mg of catalyst was distributed in tetracycline hydrochloride (20 mg L−1, 50 mL, pH 5.3). The mixture was stirred for 20 min in the dark to ensure an adsorption equilibrium. The TC solution was sampled, and its concentration was examined utilizing an ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrometer at the wavelength of 357 nm. Here C
0 and C
t are the concentration of the original TC and at irradiation time t.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer to analyze the phases of all samples (Bruker D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany). N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms were analyzed with a static volumetric adsorption analyzer (ASAP 2010 Micromeritics, USA) following the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. The morphology and crystalline lattices of the samples were obtained by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Crossbean 340, ZEISS, Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-ARM 200F, JEOL, Japan). The elemental compositions of the samples were identified with the Energy-Disperse X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) technique. Chemical groups were analyzed via Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Nexus 6700, Nicolet, USA). The elemental states were determined through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis by using a Thermo SCIENTIFIC spectrometer (Thermo Kalpha, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) (Bruins Instruments, UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer, Bruins, Germany) equipped with an integrating sphere was used to record the diffuse reflectance spectra of the samples, and BaSO4 was used as a reference with a region of 350–600 nm. The transient photocurrent responses (TPR), electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS), and Mott-Schottky (M-S) plots were gathered on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode cell (Chinstruments, China). The platinum electrode, Ag/AgCl, and the fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) coated with catalysts were utilized as the opposite electrode, reference electrode, and working electrode, respectively, and the electrolyte was a 0.5 mol/L Na2SO4 aqueous solution.

Following the hydrothermal process, the separated solids were dried and ground to produce biochar powder. As illustrated in Fig. S1, an increase in temperature and hydrothermal time resulted in the powder's color gradually deepening until it became black, indicative of heightened carbonization. The surface morphology of the biochar under varying conditions was characterized using SEM, as shown in Fig. 2 a–c. At 140 °C for 5 h, the biochar exhibited a reticulated texture. Hydrothermal temperature significantly impacts the morphology of EBC. At low temperatures (140 °C), macromolecules like cellulose in EP resist effective decomposition, leaving residues that obstruct pore channels. Insufficient gasification of internal moisture and volatile compounds further reduces pore volume. Moreover, hindered reorganization of the carbon matrix leads to mechanically weak, unstable pores prone to collapse (Peng et al., 2023). Elevated temperatures promoted the formation of particles and microporous structures on the EBC surface, as accelerated hydrolysis of macromolecular organics generated decomposition products. These products subsequently condensed into a porous carbon skeleton while releasing gases that created internal pores. XRD tests were conducted to analyze the changes in the crystal structure of the biochar under different conditions. As depicted in Fig. 2d, a spurious peak appeared near 23° at 140 °C, suggesting that low-temperature hydrothermal treatment was insufficient for complete carbonization of the EP, resulting in the presence of impurity phases. Increasing the hydrothermal time or temperature could expedite the carbonization reaction of seaweed. At 260 °C, the EBC exhibited amorphous carbon characteristics, evidenced by the enhanced intensity of the (002) peak and the disappearance of heterogeneous peaks. Extended hydrothermal treatment at elevated temperatures enhances the decomposition and polycondensation of organic macromolecules in EP. This process fragments them into smaller molecular units that randomly crosslink, generating disordered carbon networks. The structural disordering suppresses crystalline alignment, thereby intensifying the amorphous carbon characteristics observed in XRD patterns (Taher et al., 2023). Fig. S2 provides a detailed characterization of the XRD patterns of EBC under various time and temperature conditions.

Surface functional groups were confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2e). The peak at 1022 cm−1 to 1165 cm−1 results from the C-H and C-O stretching vibrations, and the stretching vibration of the C=C/C=O appears at 1631 cm−1∼1720 cm−1 (Kanjana et al., 2023; Riaz et al., 2023). The peaks at 2922 cm−1 and 3426 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of the C-H and O-H, respectively (Mojoudi et al., 2019). Characterization of the elemental composition of EBC by XPS (Fig. 2f). Three major peaks, C 1S, N 1S, and O 1S, are observed. The content of elemental C increased from 55.53 % (140 °C) to 75.26 % (260 °C), and the content of O element decreased from 29.94 % to 17.03 %, suggesting that prolonged high-temperature hydrothermal heating led to an increase in the degree of carbon skeleton formation, which breaks the C-H, C-O, and other weak bonds. Fig. 2g and h demonstrate the curve-fitted scan spectrum of C1s at different hydrothermal temperatures. The values of 284.8 eV, 286.5 eV, and 288 eV (±0.2 eV) correspond to C-C, C-O, and C=O bonds. The porous structure of EBC and abundant surface oxygen-containing groups can effectively enhance the adsorption of TC molecules. We explored the basic properties of activated carbon under different experimental conditions, followed by adsorption/desorption tests to identify suitable EBC for assisting BiOCl photodegradation (Fig. S2). Fig. 2i shows the specific surface area size (BET) and pore size distribution of EBCs under different hydrothermal conditions. The specific surface area of EBC exhibits temperature-dependent enhancement during hydrothermal processing. During initial thermal activation, partial decomposition of organic macromolecules in EP releases gaseous byproducts, triggering the formation of microporous and mesoporous frameworks that drive rapid surface area expansion. When the temperature continues to increase (140–210 °C), the carbonization and condensation reactions proceed synergistically, and the distribution of micropores and mesopores tends to be uniform. However, thermal rearrangement at 260 °C induces structural densification: pore walls undergo stress-driven shrinkage or fusion, resulting in enlarged pore diameters (Peng et al., 2023). This dual-phase evolution—initial pore development followed by high-temperature collapse—aligns with the surface area decline observed in Fig. 2i, demonstrating the thermally unstable nature of nanoscale carbon architectures under extreme hydrothermal conditions. We consider that EBC under 210 °C-10 h condition is more suitable as auxiliary materials because the medium pore size EBC can provide a large specific surface area and reduce diffusion limitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Process and mechanism of TC degradation by BiOCl@EBC
ce:section.ce:para:
Photocatalytic TC destruction experiments on the as-synthesized catalysts were performed under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the content of TC kept decreasing without desorption after 60 min of irradiation, indicating that both BiOCl and BiOCl@EBC possessed stabilizing degradation of TC. The BiOCl@EBC (5, 10, 15, 20 mg) composite respectively achieved 99.08, 98.73, 99.46, and 98.73 % tetracycline (TC) removal within 60 min, exhibiting ∼1.7-fold enhancement over pure BiOCl (57.34 %). Particularly, the BiOCl@EBC (15 mg) demonstrated 99.46 % TC degradation, implying that the incorporation of EBC was effective in enhancing the photoactivity. In addition, the treatment rate constants for five materials were determined according to a pseudo-first-order model, shown in Fig. 3b and Table S1. Strikingly, the EBC addition with 15 mg had an optimum k value of 0.084 min−1 for TC mitigation, outperforming the addition of 5 mg, 10 mg, and 20 mg. Silver nitrate (AgNO3), EDTA-2Na, tert-butanol (TBA), and ascorbic acid (VC) were each added at a concentration of 1 mmol/L to the degradation system as trapping agents for electrons (e
- ), holes (h
+ ), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), and superoxide radicals (·O
2
− ), respectively. The experimental results, presented in Fig. 3c, show that adding VC reduced the TC degradation rate to 29.08 %, suggesting that ·O
2
− is the primary free radical involved in the degradation process. Furthermore, the inclusion of EDTA-2Na and TBA reduced the TC degradation rate by 40.36 % and 68.13 %, respectively, suggesting that h
+ and ·OH contributed to the process.

Then, we characterized the surface groups of BiOCl@EBC by FTIR (Fig. 3d). The absorption peak at 519 cm−1 is assigned as the Bi-O stretching vibration (Tang et al., 2019). The peak appearing at 1642 cm−1 is attributed to the C=C stretching vibration (Kanjana et al., 2023). The intensity of the C-O stretching vibration peak around 1040 cm−1 increased gradually with higher EBC content. As shown in Fig. 3g, when BiOCl and EBC are combined, the amorphous structure of EBC will form an impurity phase in BiOCl, which reduces the XRD diffraction intensity of BiOCl itself. We observed by SEM (Fig. 3e–f) that the BiOCl showed a scale-like structure, and the introduction of EBC did not cause significant changes to its morphology due to the low doping amount. We characterized TEM EDS mapping (Fig. 3h) to determine the elemental composition of BiOCl@EBC and found that C elements were successfully loaded into the BiOCl.

To obtain an in-depth understanding of the mechanism of reinforcing the photocatalytic properties of the BiOCl@EBC, the sunlight-responsive properties and band energy-level placement of the photocatalysts were surveyed. Fig. S2 displays the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) of BiOCl and BiOCl@EBC. BiOCl@EBC (15 mg) can harvest limited amounts of visible light. Their characteristic onset-absorption edges situated at approximately 451 nm and 463 nm follow the bandgaps (Eg) of 3.45 and 3.29 eV, respectively, which were derived from the Kubelka-Munk function (as shown in Fig. 4 a). After the decoration of EBC on the BiOCl, a significant improvement in the visible-light response was detected. The superior light-capturing properties of BiOCl@EBC compared with those of BiOCl reveal its superior sunlight useability.

M-S profiles (Fig. 4b) were acquired to probe the conduction band energy levels (ECB) of BiOCl and BiOCl@EBC (15 mg) (Cai et al., 2023). BiOCl and BiOCl@EBC are n-type semiconductors, owing to their positive slopes. The flat-band positions (Efb) of BiOCl and BiOCl@EBC were −0.257 V and −0.228 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), equal to −0.06 V and −0.031 V (vs. normal hydrogen electrode; NHE), with the aid of the formula ENHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.197 V. For the n-type catalysts, ECB was higher than Efb by approximately 0.1 eV. Correspondingly, the ECB values of BiOCl and BiOCl@EBC were separately −0.16 V and −0.13 V (vs. NHE), using the equation EVB = Eg + ECB, the EVB values were defined as 3.29 V and 3.16 V (vs. NHE). The energy-level distribution of BiOCl and BiOCl@EBC (15 mg) is shown in Fig. 4c. The LSV curve (Fig. 4d) showed that the photocurrent of EBC was higher than that of BiOCl at re-positive voltage, implying that the introduction of EBC could enhance the catalytic efficiency. Fundamentally, an improvement in photocarrier disintegration induces higher TPR intensity (Li et al., 2023b). Photoelectrochemical analyses were performed to study photocarrier detachment and migration kinetics of the BiOCl and BiOCl@EBC. As shown in Fig. 4e, the TPR intensity of BiOCl@EBC far exceeded those of the BiOCl, indicating effectively optimized photocarrier detachment and transport in the BiOCl@EBC. Furthermore, the EIS data were analyzed to elucidate the charge-transport behavior (Li et al., 2023a). Enhanced charge-carrier detachment results in a small arc radius. As shown in Fig. 4f, the arc radius of the BiOCl@EBC was appreciably reduced compared to those of the BiOCl, revealing a remarkably boosted charge transport in the BiOCl@EBC. Electrical measurements indicated that EBCs as electron acceptors could accelerate the electron transfer of BiOCl. The degradation process is shown in Fig. 4g, electron-rich EBC enhanced the adsorption sites and properties of BiOCl, which enhanced the mass transfer of TC molecules, accelerated the reaction kinetics, and facilitated the efficient separation of electron-hole pairs (Kang et al., 2023). Upon exposure to light, a TC solution containing BiOCl@EBC composites generates photogenerated electron-hole pairs, as described in (Equation (2)). The electrons (e
- ) generated in the conduction band (CB) of the composite are likely to migrate to the surface of EBC, where they can function as electron acceptors. These transferred from the conduction band of BiOCl can engage in reduction reactions. Specifically, as outlined in (Equation (3)), these electrons can reduce O2 to produce superoxide radicals (∙O
2
− ). Concurrently, holes (h
+ ) are generated in the valence band of BiOCl (Equation (4)) and can react with water molecules (H2O) to yield hydroxyl radicals (∙OH), as shown in (Equation (5)). The ∙OH, ∙O
2
− , and h
+ can subsequently interact with TC molecules, leading to a series of oxidation reactions that culminate in the degradation of TC into CO2, H2O, and various small molecules. Furthermore, the incorporation of EBC enhances the adsorption of TC molecules onto its surface, thereby facilitating a rapid electron transfer pathway. The main mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of TC involves absorption, separation, and migration of photogenerated carriers and subsequent redox reactions (Wei et al., 2020):
(2) BiOCl@EBC + h

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of degradation intermediates and toxicity assessment
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the photocatalytic degradation pathways of TC over BiOCl@EBC composites in depth, LC-MS analysis was employed to identify reaction intermediates after 40 min of illumination, with corresponding MS spectra displayed in Fig. S6 and Table S2. By analyzing intermediate products based on mass-to-charge ratios, probable degradation pathways were established (Fig. 5 a). The degradation intermediates identified in this study exhibited highly fragmented characteristics (m/z < 250), distinct from previous reports (Guo et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2024), potentially attributable to the unique properties of BiOCl@EBC. The degradation process initiated with C-ring cleavage of TC, subsequently diverging into two concurrent pathways (Pathway I and Pathway II).

In Pathway I, structural stability of the benzene ring directed initial degradation toward the branched chain of the B-ring (cleaved C-ring product P1). Through sequential oxidation, demethylation, and ring-opening reactions, P1 was ultimately converted to P5 (m/z = 122). Pathway II involved modifications at the amino and methyl groups on the D-ring. Subsequent dehydroxylation, demethylation, deamination, and dehydrogenation transformed P6 into P7 (m/z = 130) and P10 (m/z = 159). These intermediates further underwent deoxygenation, dehydrogenation, and carbon chain cleavage to form P8 (m/z = 127) and P11 (m/z = 118). Progressive oxidation converted these intermediates into low-molecular-weight organic groups (m/z = 74 and 186), ultimately mineralizing into H2O, CO2, NH4 +, and other inorganic compounds (Xin et al., 2021). Unlike conventional TC degradation primarily occurring at the D-ring (Dou et al., 2025; Guo et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2021), this dual-pathway mechanism significantly enhanced degradation efficiency.

Toxicity evaluation using QSAR-based T.E.S.T software assessed acute toxicity through three parameters: Fathead minnow LC50 (96 h), developmental toxicity, and mutagenicity (Table S2, Fig. 5b–d). The original TC exhibited higher toxicity (LC50 = 0.90 mg L−1) than all degradation intermediates (Fig. 5b). While TC was classified as a developmental toxicant (Fig. 5c), photocatalytic treatment reduced toxicity, with P7 and P9 becoming developmentally non-toxic. Mutagenicity analysis (Fig. 5d) demonstrated that most small-molecule intermediates, except P2 and P3, tested negative for mutagenicity. Although residual mutagenicity and toxicity persisted in some intermediates, overall toxicity was substantially reduced compared to parent TC. Prolonged reaction time could further degrade these residual toxic intermediates, ultimately eliminating TC-related environmental risks.

We extracted a hydrothermal solution to prepare carbon dots. Large size particles and impurities were present in the untreated solution (as shown in Fig. S2). We purified the carbon dot solution by centrifugation and filtration, and the TEM shown in Fig. 6 a shows that the purified carbon dots are significantly smaller in size (∼18 nm) and uniformly distributed.

FTIR characterization was performed to obtain more information on the structure of the ECDs. Fig. 6b shows that the broad absorption peak at 3420 cm−1 corresponds to the characteristic absorption bands of -OH vibration (Tan and Zhou, 2024), whereas the two peaks detected at approximately 2940 cm−1 and 2855 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of C-H bonds (Hu et al., 2020). The peaks noted at 1635 cm−1 and 1060 cm−1 are linked to the vibrations of C=O/C=C and C-O bonds, respectively (Riaz et al., 2023). The elements and surface analysis of ECDs are analyzed by XPS characterization (Fig. 6c) and we found that they contain carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen elements. The C 1s spectra (Fig. 6d) indicate the presence of three different types of carbon bonds: graphitic or aliphatic (C=C and C−C) (284.3 eV), and C-O (286.3 eV) (Zhang et al., 2023a). The two N 1S signals (Fig. 6e), corresponding to C-N (400 eV) and graphitic N (401.5 eV), are also seen. The 531.5 eV signal (Fig. 6f) is attributed to the C=O.

Optical properties of ECDs characterized by PL and UV–vis spectroscopy. The water solution of ECDs (Fig. S2) exhibited an absorption peak around 340 nm attributed to the n-π∗ transition induced by C-O bonds. We characterized the PL of the ECDs aqueous solutions at various hydrothermal temperatures and times (as shown in Fig. S4 and Fig. S5), which all showed obvious excitation-dependent behavior. As shown in Fig. 7 a–b, upon increasing the excitation wavelength from 320 to 400 nm, the emission peak of ECDs red-shifted from 400 to 500 nm, which exhibited a clear PL excitation-dependent behavior under different temperatures. The surface functional groups and size of the carbon dots are the major factors inducing this behavior (Goswami et al., 2022). During hydrothermal reaction is stochastic, and the formation of carbon nuclei affects the size of the carbon dots, which consequently affects the optical band gap (Xu et al., 2004). In addition, capture sites at different energy levels are induced by oxygen-containing functional groups, forming capture centers for defective sites on the surface of ECDs. Specific excitation wavelengths activate these emission trap sites, resulting in excitation-dependent emission (Wang et al., 2021). We observed the influence of temperature and time on ECDs by the normalized PL emission peaks of ECDs under 340 nm excitation (as shown in Fig. 7d). Clearly, raising the temperature or increasing the hydrothermal time resulted in a shift of the PL emission peaks towards shorter wavelengths. The color change of the ECDs solution under a UV lamp also demonstrates the phenomenon of shifted emission peaks. As shown in Fig. 7e, the color of the ECDs was light green at 140 °C, and the color gradually appeared blue with the increase of the hydrothermal temperature (from 140 °C to 260 °C). We observed the PLQY (Fig. 7c) of the synthesized ECDs solutions under different conditions and found that elevating the hydrothermal temperature or prolonging the reaction time could enhance the PLQY of ECDs. The excellent PLQY indicated that the material has a higher radiative compounding efficiency, which depends on the size of the ECDs and the surface groups. Higher temperatures accelerate the rate of decomposition and carbonization of organic matter in EP, which leads to the creation of smaller carbon nuclei (Wang et al., 2021). In addition, the rapid nucleation process reduces the aggregation effect, resulting in the formation of ECDs with a uniform size distribution.

CDs are typically employed for patterning by first preparing carbon dot ink and then printing the desired pattern using a printer. While this method is effective for creating intricate anti-counterfeiting patterns, it is often time-consuming and necessitates a specific concentration of carbon dot ink. To enhance efficiency in the utilization of carbon dots, we opted for a spray-based patterning approach. In this method, the purified aqueous solution of carbon dots is directly diluted with pure water to create a low-concentration solution (30 mg/500 mL) suitable for spraying. The process begins by placing the prepared mask onto the cotton fabric, followed by spraying carbon dots onto the mask using a spray gun to create a customized pattern on the fabric, as illustrated in Fig. 8 a. Under indoor lighting conditions, the cotton woven fabric does not reveal any pattern; however, under UV light, the word "NKU" becomes visible, as depicted in Fig. 8b. Remarkably, the pattern size can be as large as 10 × 10 cm, and the spraying time is efficiently reduced to just 15 s. We propose that this method holds significant potential for commercialization, particularly in applications such as the production of brochures and the patterning of greeting cards. Fig. 8c illustrates a combination of security arrays composed of various numbers, demonstrating the potential for diverse configurations. To achieve more intricate patterns, a complex mask plate, as shown in Fig. 8d, was employed during the spraying process. This method facilitated the creation of refined patterns. Surprisingly, the cost of each pattern remained low (10 cents per pattern), even after accounting for raw materials and utilities. As depicted in Fig. 8e, the ECDs pattern applied to cotton fabric via spraying exhibits excellent ductility. The utilization of random numbers and complex patterns is particularly effective for anti-counterfeiting purposes. Furthermore, the flexibility of the coating method allows these anti-counterfeiting patterns to be applied to a wide range of irregular surfaces. Consequently, this significantly broadens the application scenarios for ECDs in anti-counterfeiting measures.

In summary, this study introduces a straightforward and efficient HMP strategy (one stone) that simultaneously tackles three objectives (three birds): processing waste EP; modifying the energy bands and electrical properties of BiOCl; rapidly generating fluorescent anticounterfeit patterns. The facile HMP strategy was employed to extract fluorescent ECDs and EBC from EP, demonstrating an effective strategy for the efficient utilization of hydrothermal products and expanding the diversity of EP processing. We investigated the effects of hydrothermal temperature and time on the properties of ECDs synthesized with uniform size distribution and excellent fluorescence stability. The successful synthesis of these ECDs paved the way for the preparation of low-cost, flexible anti-counterfeiting patterns by spraying the ECDs solution, showcasing the commercialization potential of this innovative approach. Additionally, we prepared BiOCl@EBC composites by leveraging the porous structure and abundant surface oxygen-containing functional groups of EBC. The BiOCl@EBC was utilized to degrade TC, achieving an enhanced degradation efficiency from 57.34 % to 99.46 % due to the optimization of the energy band structure and electrical properties of BiOCl by EBC. Toxicity assessments suggest that most degradation products of TC exhibit low toxicity or are non-toxic, indicating that they are not to pose a threat to aquatic ecosystems. While this study focused on establishing the fundamental BiOCl@EBC synergy for TC degradation, we acknowledge that extending this approach to diverse catalyst architectures requires systematic exploration in future studies. Our findings highlight biochar's dual role as both performance enhancer additive in photocatalytic systems, with promising implications for developing eco-friendly remediation technologies. The HMP strategy's versatility in biomass valorization is demonstrated through its closed-loop design integrating waste conversion and functional material synthesis. Current limitations regarding hazardous byproduct management and process optimization (drying processes employed in HMP lead to energy consumption and carbon emissions) are recognized as critical challenges for industrial translation, which are being addressed in our ongoing efforts through advanced hydrothermal engineering and lifecycle analysis.
